Diagnosis can be confirmed by ragged red fibers on muscle biopsy and mitochondrial crystalline inclusions on electron microscopy. This patient's acute onset of ophthalmoplegia and lack of a compatible family history are not consistent with this diagnosis.
Paramedian pontine infarction (Choice D) can affect the abducens and facial nerves, the medial longitudinal fasciculus, and the corticospinal tract, leading to impaired abduction of the ipsilateral eye, ipsilateral facial paresis, internuclear ophthalmoplegia (impaired adduction of the ipsilateral eye and contralateral abduction saccade), and contralateral hemiparesis. Exophthalmos and a history of eye pain are not consistent with this diagnosis.
Posterior communicating artery aneurysm (Choice E) may compress the ipsilateral oculomotor cranial nerve, resulting in ptosis, exotropia and hypotropia (lateral and inferior pupil position), diplopia, and mydriasis. The detection of a new oculomotor nerve palsy is a medical emergency, as aneurysmal rupture can lead to life-threatening subarachnoid hemorrhage.
Type 2 diabetes mellitus (Choice F) causes microvascular damage to a variety of tissues, including nerves, and can cause cranial nerve palsy. Microvascular abducens nerve palsy presents with esotropia, unilateral abduction deficit, and horizontal gaze diplopia. Exophthalmos is not associated with microvascular nerve palsy.
explanation.educationalObjective: Educational Objective: Cavernous sinus thrombosis is a potentially life-threatening complication of an infection or injury involving a structure drained by the cavernous sinus. It presents with cranial nerve deficits affecting the oculomotor, trochlear, abducens, and trigeminal nerves, along with exophthalmos, periorbital edema, fever, meningismus, and papilledema. Treatment includes intravenous broad-spectrum antibiotics and anticoagulants.

text: A previously healthy 3-month-old boy is brought to the physician because of a 3-day history of decreased feeding. He also has a 1-day history of cough that worsens when he attempts to feed. His mother reports that he seems hungry but "tires out" while feeding. He was born at 40 weeks' gestation to a 25-year-old primigravid woman following an uncomplicated pregnancy and delivery. He weighed 3600 g (7 lb 15 oz) at birth. Until 1 week ago, he had been exclusively breast-fed; he now receives cow milk-based formula. The patient's father has been excavating the soil around the house to build a foundation for an additional room. On examination, the patient is alert and quiet and has a weak cry. He is at the 50th percentile for length and weight. His temperature is 37.2°C (99°F), pulse is 138/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 72/48 mm Hg. Examination of the skin shows no abnormalities. Facial expression is absent. The pupils are 5 mm and sluggishly reactive. The gag reflex is decreased. Transmitted upper airway noises are heard. No murmurs are heard. Abdominal examination shows no abnormalities. Peripheral pulses are 2+ and symmetric. There is head lag and no spontaneous movement of the extremities. Muscle tone is decreased. Deep tendon reflexes are normal. Which of the following is most likely to confirm the diagnosis?

explanation.correct:
Correct Answer: A.

This patient with difficulty feeding, hypotonia, and facial muscle weakness has a presentation concerning for infantile botulism. Botulism is an acute disease of the neuromuscular junction caused by exposure to the Clostridium botulinum toxin. C. botulinum is a gram-positive, spore-forming, obligate anaerobic bacterium. It produces botulinum toxin, a potent neurotoxin that is absorbed into the bloodstream and transported to the neuromuscular junction where it interferes with the release of acetylcholine, thereby causing weakness and paralysis. Botulism is often encountered as a food-borne illness through contaminated food products. Historically, C. botulinum was responsible for outbreaks of "sausage poisoning" through contaminated ham products and acquired its name after the Latin name for sausage, "botulus." In the modern era, it is more often responsible for poisoning via the ingestion of raw honey or canned foods in infants. Infection more commonly occurs through inhalation of spores from contaminated dirt or dust, however, a common mode of exposure is the ingestion of spores in honey, a food that infants should avoid. The incubation period is typically hours to sufficiently mature to produce detectable botulism. Patients typically present with progressive, symmetric hypotonia and hyporeflexia that begins with the cranial nerves, manifesting as ptosis, sluggish pupils, and a diminished gag reflex, and descends to the lower extremities, also causing constipation, poor feeding, and respiratory failure. Diagnosis is confirmed by identification of the botulinum toxin in the stool. Treatment requires hospitalization and administration of immune globulin. As a precautionary measure, the patient's caregivers should be asked about exposure to honey. Infants should not be fed honey.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answers: B, C, D, and E.

Measurement of serum cholinesterase activity (Choice B) can be performed in suspected cases of organophosphate toxicity. Patients classically present with diarrhea, urination, miosis, bronchospasm, bradycardia, vomiting, lacrimation, and salivation.

Measurement of negative inspiratory force (NIF) (Choice C) can be performed to assess respiratory muscle strength in patients with muscular weakness. A low NIF may indicate that a patient is at risk for respiratory muscle fatigue and decompensation and may necessitate respiratory support. Patients with botulism may develop diaphragmatic weakness and therefore may have a low NIF, but this is a nonspecific finding and is not diagnostic for any one disease. Additionally, measurement of NIF requires patient participation (patient is instructed to fully inhale), which would not be possible for this 3-month-old patient.

Muscle biopsy (Choice D) is a part of the workup for botulism. Biopsy should be normal in patients with botulism, as it is a disease of the neuromuscular junction, not the muscle itself.

Electromyography and nerve conduction studies (Choice E) and repetitive nerve stimulation study (Choice F) can be done to support the diagnosis of botulism, but the gold standard is the stool toxin assay. The diagnosis is supported by a characteristic low amplitude response on repetitive nerve stimulation studies (electromyography) that improves with repeated stimulation.
explanation.educationalObjective: Educational Objective: Infantile botulism is caused by the ingestion of Clostridium botulinum spores in infants. Patients typically present with progressive, symmetric hypotonia, hyporeflexia, cranial nerve palsies, and respiratory failure. Diagnosis is confirmed by identification of the botulinum toxin in the stool. Treatment requires hospitalization and administration of immune globulin.

text: A 9-year-old girl is brought to the emergency department 30 minutes after being involved in a motor vehicle collision. There is an open fracture of the tibial shaft, and the patient reports severe pain in the area surrounding the fracture. On examination, there is weakness of toe flexion, severe pain with passive extension of the toes, and plantar hyperesthesia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.correct:
Correct Answer: A.

Compartment syndrome occurs when there is increased pressure in the intramuscular compartment that compromises circulation and neuromuscular and neurologic function. Compartment syndrome commonly occurs in the setting of trauma, including long bone fractures, and symptoms include pain out of proportion to the injury, paresthesia, and pain with passive stretching of the muscles. Late findings are pallor, pulselessness, and paralysis. Diagnosis is confirmed after clinical suspicion by measuring compartment pressures. Increased compartment pressure or the presence of acute compartment syndrome and correlating blood pressures confirm the diagnosis. Patients with compartment syndrome are treated with emergent fasciotomy to decompress involved compartments; delays may lead to poor outcomes.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answers: B, C, D, and E.
Complex regional pain syndrome (Choice B), or reflex sympathetic dystrophy, presents with regional pain that is out of proportion to the insult. Other symptoms include edema, abnormal sensation, swelling, and erythema. The most common inciting event for complex regional pain syndrome is a soft tissue injury.
Deep venous thrombosis (Choice C) causes extremity swelling, pain, and discoloration. Symptoms may be acute or subacute in nature, and patients are treated with anticoagulants. This patient's clinical presentation is not consistent with deep venous thrombosis.
Ruptured posterior tibial artery (Choice D) may result from posterior knee dislocation, direct trauma, or aneurysmal disease. This injury is usually the result of high-impact trauma, such as a motor vehicle collision. Patients may present with abnormal pulses and pain. Rapid identification of this injury, imaging with angiography, and repair of the injured vessel must be completed promptly to salvage the affected limb.
Transection of the tibial nerve (Choice E) may occur in the case of high-impact trauma or during surgical procedures. Symptoms may differ depending on the level of injury. This patient's symptoms, including pain out of proportion to the injury, are more suggestive of compartment syndrome.
explanation.educationalObjective: Educational Objective: Compartment syndrome may arise in the setting of trauma and is associated with severe pain, paresthesia, and pain with passive movement. As vascular compromise worsens, patients may experience pallor, pulselessness, and paralysis. Diagnosis is made by measurement of compartment pressures, and patients are treated with emergent fasciotomy.

text: An 18-year-old woman comes to the office to request genetic testing. She was adopted at birth, and her adoptive parents were told that there was a strong maternal history of psychotic behavior and a movement disorder. The patient has been unable to obtain any further medical information. She has no history of serious illness. She takes no medications. She is planning to start college in the fall and has been an excellent student throughout high school. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. There is no evidence of abnormal movements. Mental status examination shows an anxious mood. She reports no hallucinations, and there is no evidence of delusions. After further counseling, the patient elects to proceed with genetic testing. Testing for which of the following is most appropriate at this time?

explanation.correct:
Correct Answer: C.

Based on the reported maternal history of psychotic and movement symptoms, this patient should be offered testing for Huntington disease (HD). HD is a hereditary, progressive neurodegenerative disorder typically arising in the 3rd to 6th decades of life associated with atrophy of the caudate nuclei in the basal ganglia, which can be seen on MRI of the brain. The disease usually presents with an insidious onset of psychiatric (irritability, depression, psychosis) and cognitive symptoms. Motor symptoms (chorea, hypotonia, dystonia, loss of voluntary control of movements) typically appear later in the disease course. Patients with HD typically die 10 to 40 years after disease onset. Chorea, a key HD symptom, can be initially experienced as restlessness. Tremor, muscle spasms, and other involuntary movements may also occur. The key HD gene is located on chromosome 4, which occurs when a series of three nucleotides in repetition are replicated incorrectly and too many repeats are included in the replicated product. It is inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern and also demonstrates anticipation, a genetic inheritance phenomenon in which future generations are affected at a younger age or with more severe symptoms. HD is diagnosed clinically and with genetic testing, which measures the number of trinucleotide repeats in the HD gene.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answers: A, B, D, and E.
Fragile X syndrome (Choice A) is an X-linked dominant cause of inherited intellectual disability that presents due to a CGG-trinucleotide repeat expansion in the FMR1 gene. It presents with clinical features including intellectual disability, an elongated face with a prominent jaw, a high-arched palate, large ears, hyperflexible joints, and postpubertal macroorchidism. These features are less consistent with this patient's reported family history.
Hepatolenticular degeneration (Wilson disease) (Choice B) is caused by impaired metabolism of copper and deposition in the liver and brain. Most patients will have some degree of liver disease at the time of diagnosis. Neurologic and psychiatric manifestations are broad and can mimic other neurologic disorders, especially ones with movement-related symptoms. Patients with neurologic abnormalities will almost always have Kayser-Fleischer rings. This patient lacks features of liver disease. HD disease should be distinguished from Wilson disease from similar disorders. Initial testing involves measurement of serum ceruloplasmin concentrations.
Parkinson disease (Choice D) is characterized by resting tremor, rigidity, bradykinesia, and postural instability. Patients with PD rarely present with psychotic behavior and the disorder is not inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern. While anxiety and depression are common in PD patients, psychosis is rare and is usually a result of medications used in treatment.
Porphyria (Choice E) is a group of inherited metabolic disorders that can cause episodic neurologic symptoms, abdominal pain, and psychological symptoms. The most common acute porphyria is acute intermittent porphyria, which is caused by defects in enzymes involved in heme synthesis. Porphyria does not typically cause movement disorders.
explanation.educationalObjective: Educational Objective: Huntington disease (HD) is a hereditary, progressive neurodegenerative disorder associated with atrophy of the caudate nuclei in the basal ganglia. It is caused by a trinucleotide repeat expansion in the HD gene located on chromosome 4 and is inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern. Patients present with a combination of psychiatric, cognitive, and motor symptoms.

text: A 27-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 1-year history of pain, weakness, and numbness in both arms and hands. Examination shows weakness and atrophy of the muscles of the arms and hands bilaterally. There is loss of sensation to pain and temperature over the neck, shoulders, and upper extremities but preservation of sensation to light touch. Deep tendon reflexes are absent in the upper extremities. He has thoracic kyphoscoliosis. Neurologic examination of the lower extremities and cranial nerves shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.correct:
Correct Answer: I.

This patient presenting with a lower motor neuron pattern of dysfunction in the upper extremities with a syringomyelia likely is a result of syringomyelia. Syringomyelia features a cystic cavity in the central cervical or thoracic spinal cord, most commonly in the cervical or thoracic spinal cord. Syringomyelia may be associated with congenital malformations such as Chiari malformation type I, spinal cord infections, autoimmune diseases such as multiple sclerosis, neoplasms, or spinal cord trauma. Syringomyelia typically compresses spinothalamic fibers decussating in the anterior white commissure. Consequently, patients present with deficits in pain and temperature sensation in the bilateral upper extremities and/or torso. (Since the ascending spinothalamic tracts in the lateral spinal cord are unaffected, sensation in the lower body is intact.) Neuropathic pain in the upper extremities may occur. The lesion can expand to compress the bilateral anterior horns (the location of the cell bodies of lower motoneurons). Therefore, patients may present with a lower motoneuron pattern of dysfunction in the bilateral upper extremities (eg, decreased muscle tone, hyporeflexia, early muscle atrophy). The cyst may progressively expand to compress the lateral corticospinal tract in severe cases, leading to an upper motoneuron pattern of dysfunction in the lower body.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answers: A, B, C, D, E, F, G, H, and J.
Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (Choice A) is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder that affects the anterior horns and the lateral corticospinal tract as it descends in the spinal cord. Subsequently, patients demonstrate a combination of upper and lower motoneuron findings in an asymmetric distribution that may include bulbar muscles. Sensory impairment is atypical, and a pattern of dysfunction that can be localized to a single lesion within the cervical or thoracic spinal cord is more consistent with syringomyelia.
Cerebellar degeneration (Choice B) can occur with chronic alcohol use, nutritional deficiency, or as a paraneoplastic disorder and presents with ataxia, gait instability, dysarthria, and nystagmus, and weakness of all extremities. Gait instability and weakness of the lower extremities with upper motor neuron characteristics are common early symptoms.
Cervical spondylosis (Choice C) refers to gradual, age-related degenerative changes of the cervical spine and presents with neck pain, radiculopathy, or myelopathy.
Friedreich ataxia (Choice D) is a genetic condition that presents with ataxia, spasticity, and impaired speech typically in young children or adolescents. It is associated with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.
Huntington disease (Choice E) is a hereditary, progressive neurodegenerative disorder that presents with psychiatric, cognitive, and motor symptoms.
Muscular dystrophy (Choice F) refers to a group of inherited myopathies and presents with symmetric proximal muscle weakness in the absence of upper motoneuron signs.
Spinal cord compression (Choice G) can occur as a result of trauma or compression from tumors or infections. Symptoms range from myelopathy and sensory changes to complete paralysis.
Subacute combined degeneration (Choice H) occurs as a result of vitamin B₁₂ deficiency and affects the spinocerebellar tracts, lateral corticospinal tracts, and dorsal columns, causing weakness, paresthesia, and loss of proprioception and vibration sense.
Thoracic outlet syndrome (Choice J) is a disorder of the brachial plexus and subclavian vessels that can result in symptoms of pain, numbness, and weakness in the upper extremity.
explanation.educationalObjective: Educational Objective: Syringomyelia features a cystic cavity in the central cervical or thoracic spinal cord, which affects midline structures such as the anterior white commissure (decussation of the spinothalamic tracts) and the anterior horns (cell bodies of lower motoneurons). Patients with syringomyelia may present with deficits in pain and temperature sensation and a lower motoneuron pattern of dysfunction in the bilateral upper extremities and/or torso.

text: An 85-year-old man with a 4-year history of amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) is evaluated in the intensive care unit (ICU) 3 weeks after admission for treatment of respiratory failure. Prior to admission, the patient had experienced progressive weakness since ALS was diagnosed, to the point where he was unable to swallow or speak. After admission to the ICU, he underwent tracheostomy and mechanical ventilation. Since admission, four attempts to wean the patient from the ventilator have failed. During today's evaluation, the patient is awake and remains unable to answer any questions correctly. He has little voluntary movement of the chest wall. Physical examination discloses diffuse atrophy, prominent fasciculations, marked weakness in all extremities, and diffuse hyperreflexia. Prior to admission, he told his family that he did not want extreme measures utilized to keep him alive. The patient does not have a living will, but his daughter has medical durable power of attorney for the patient. Which of the following is the most appropriate recommendation for the physician to give this patient's family?

options.A: Ask the daughter to make a decision on discontinuation of care
options.B: Consider stem cell therapy for treatment of ALS
options.C: Continue supportive care, administer antibiotic therapy, and attempt to wean the patient from the ventilator
options.E: Request a consultation with the hospital ethics committee

explanation.correct:
Correct Answer: A.

This patient has expressed a preference regarding end-of-life care prior to his current illness but is currently incapacitated and unable to express a preference regarding his care. Options for directing care in a state of incapacity include durable powers of attorney or advance directives, which enable patients to dictate their preferences for end-of-life care ahead of time or delegate these responsibilities to a designated person. A power of attorney is a legal document by which an individual authorizes another person to make decisions on their behalf regarding medical care. This can be either for medical care or financial matters. In this case, the patient's daughter has medical durable power of attorney (DPOA) for the patient, meaning she is legally authorized to make healthcare decisions on his behalf when he is incapacitated. Therefore, she should make the decision regarding discontinuation of care.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answers: B, C, D, and E.
Consider stem cell therapy for treatment of ALS (Choice B) is incorrect. Consideration of experimental treatments would be inappropriate given this patient's advanced state of disease and current respiratory failure.
Continuing supportive care, administering antibiotic therapy, and attempting to wean the patient from the ventilator (Choice C) is unlikely to be successful given previous failed attempts to wean off the ventilator and given this patient's lack of spontaneous respirations. Discussing discontinuation of care with his designated power of attorney is more appropriate given his overall prognosis.
Discontinuing the ventilator today (Choice D) is inappropriate without approval from the patient's designated decision-maker.
Requesting a consultation with the hospital ethics committee (Choice E) may be necessary if there is conflict between a patient's previously stated wishes or advance directive and the decision of a power of attorney or next of kin but would be unnecessary at this time.
explanation.educationalObjective: Educational Objective: A power of attorney grants a particular person authority to act on the patient's behalf should the patient become incapacitated and is one option to direct end-of-life decision-making ahead of time should a patient become unable to direct their own care. When a patient is incapacitated, decision-making regarding their care is made by the power of attorney or by an advance directive.

text: A 32-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 6-month history of daily generalized headaches. She occasionally has blurred vision in the right eye during a headache. Treatment with ibuprofen relieves the headaches. She has had a 23-kg (50-lb) weight gain and menstrual irregularity over the past year. Menses now occur at irregular 20- to 90-day intervals. She is 168 cm (5 ft 6 in) tall and weighs 82 kg (180 lb); BMI is 29 kg/m². Examination shows a visual acuity of 20/20 in both eyes. The visual fields are normal. Optic discs are indistinct, and spontaneous venous pulsations are not observed. The remainder of the neurologic examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.correct:
Correct Answer: A.

Idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH) is primarily driven by impaired resorption of cerebrospinal fluid. Signs of increased intracranial pressure (ICP) include headache, nausea, vomiting, and transient visual obscurations (blurring), and may be secondary to pressure on the optic nerve or optic chiasm. Evaluation findings include papilledema and increased opening pressure on lumbar puncture without any underlying pathology to cause such findings on neuroimaging. Although there is no malignant cause underlying the increased ICP, it may cause debilitating headaches and permanent vision loss. Headaches that are secondary to increased ICP often are worse when lying down and relieved by standing. The most common risk factors for the development of pseudotumor cerebri include female gender and obesity. However, hypervitaminosis A is another potential cause of IIH. This can be from excessive dietary intake or from the use of retinoid medications such as isotretinoin or all-trans retinoic acid. Other medications associated with IIH include tetracyclines, danazol, and growth hormone.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answers: B, C, D, and E.
Pituitary tumor (Choice B) can present with headache and either hypopituitarism or hyperpituitarism, depending on whether the tumor is nonfunctional or functional in nature. Compression of the adjacent optic chiasm causes bitemporal hemianopsia, not unilateral blurred vision.
Status migrainosus (Choice C) is a refractory migraine attack persisting for over 72 hours. Treatment includes intravenous nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, such as ketorolac, as well as dopamine receptor blockers, such as metoclopramide or prochlorperazine.
Subdural hematoma (Choice D) may result from head trauma and hemorrhage from bridging veins in the subdural space. Evidence of increased ICP such as postural headache and papilledema are present, as in this patient, but progressive neurologic decline will also occur. On imaging, it appears as a crescent-shaped fluid collection abutting the internal surface of the skull, not bound by suture lines.
Tension-type headache (Choice E) typically presents with bilateral, dull, pressure-like or band-like pain across the frontal and temporal areas, often arising from stress. It does not present with postural headache, papilledema, visual deficits, or other neurologic signs.
explanation.educationalObjective: Educational Objective: Impaired resorption of cerebrospinal fluid is the mechanism behind idiopathic intracranial hypertension, which is characterized by headache, papilledema, and increased opening pressure on lumbar puncture without any underlying pathology to cause such findings on neuroimaging. It is primarily found in young women who are overweight.

text: A 7-year-old girl is brought to the physician by her mother because of dull, generalized headache for the past 2 days. During the past week, the patient has had difficulty walking and frequently has had loss of balance. She has no history of serious illness, and her growth and development have been appropriate for age. Her temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 80/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 100/75 mm Hg. Physical examination shows tilting of the head to the left. There is marked horizontal nystagmus on lateral gaze bilaterally. Visual field testing shows no abnormalities, and visual acuity is 20/20 in both eyes. Finger-nose testing shows past-pointing bilaterally. She has an ataxic gait, and there is in-toeing of the left foot. Romberg sign is present. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.E: Tumor of the vestibulocochlear cranial nerve

explanation.correct:
Correct Answer: A.

This patient with headache, ataxia, dysmetria, and nystagmus has a presentation concerning for a brain stem glioma. Gliomas are the most common pediatric brain tumor and are typically low-grade pilocytic astrocytomas. Pediatric gliomas commonly grow in the cerebellum but can occur in the brain stem. Patients typically present with focal neurologic deficits caused by mass effect that may progress over the course of months as a result of the tumor growing larger and affecting additional brain regions. Treatment centers around radiation and chemotherapy for brain stem gliomas.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answers: B, C, D, and E.

Craniopharyngioma (Choice B) is a rare pituitary mass derived from remnants of the Rathke pouch. Due to its proximity to the optic chiasm, patients classically present with bitemporal hemianopsia, but a horizontal nystagmus on lateral gaze as found in this patient would be very unusual.

Great vein of Galen aneurysm (Choice C) is a rare form of arteriovenous malformation that affects one of the intracranial veins. Patients may present with signs and symptoms of heart failure, hydrocephalus, or headache.

Meningitis (Choice D) may present with a headache, neck pain, and fever. This patient's lack of fever in addition to numerous focal neurologic deficits point to an alternative diagnosis.

Tumor of the vestibulocochlear cranial nerve (Choice E), such as an acoustic neuroma, can cause ipsilateral hearing loss and vertigo, and if also compressing the trigeminal nerve, can lead to ipsilateral facial numbness and paresthesia.
explanation.educationalObjective: Educational Objective: Gliomas are the most common pediatric brain tumor and are typically low-grade pilocytic astrocytomas that may occur in the cerebellum or brain stem. Gliomas commonly present with focal neurologic deficits caused by mass effect that may progress over the course of months as a result of the tumor growing larger and affecting additional brain regions.

text:
A 71-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of a 2-month history of increasingly severe, aching pain in her shoulders, upper arms, and thighs. She says, "I can't roll over in bed any longer and my husband has to assist me in getting up from a chair." She also has had associated muscle stiffness that lasts 1 hour every morning upon awakening; the stiffness sometimes occurs in the afternoon following periods of inactivity. She also reports awakening multiple times during the night, diffuse joint pain, and a 7.7-kg (17-lb) weight loss despite no change in appetite. Menopause occurred at age 56 years. She also has osteoporosis and generalized anxiety disorder. Her medications are estradiol, escitalopram, and vitamin D and calcium supplementation. Her mother and aunt had rheumatoid arthritis, and her brother had sclerosing cholangitis. The patient appears to be in moderate discomfort. She is 174 cm (5 ft 8 in) tall and weighs 64 kg (140 lb); BMI is 21 kg/m³. Temperature is 36.3°C (97.3°F), pulse is 68/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 124/76 mm Hg. Muscle strength and tone are normal. She is unable to actively abduct the shoulders bilaterally past 90 degrees because of stiffness. Passive range of motion of the shoulders and hips is decreased. There is no pain on palpation of the temporalis muscles. Results of laboratory studies are shown:
Hemoglobin 8.9 g/dL
Hematocrit 25%
Leukocyte count 8200/mm³
Platelet count 572,000/mm³
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 82 mm/h
Serum
C-reactive protein 15.3 mg/L
Creatine kinase 112 U/L
Electromyography and nerve conduction studies disclose no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.B: Biopsy of the vastus medialis muscle

explanation.correct:
Correct Answer: F.

Polymyalgia rheumatica (PMR) is an inflammatory condition characterized by proximal muscle and joint (eg, shoulder and hip girdle) pain, stiffness, and increased inflammatory markers, including erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. Untreated, muscle weakness may result from disuse atrophy. Systemic symptoms such as fatigue and anorexia are associated with PMR. PMR is a diagnosis of exclusion. Symptoms of giant cell arteritis, such as through unilateral headache, vision changes, or jaw claudication, should prompt temporal artery biopsy. The diagnosis of PMR is based on clinical features, and treatment is with prednisone for up to 1 year. It is common for patients to respond to prednisone almost immediately; thus, the absence of response should prompt consideration of a different diagnosis.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answers: A, B, C, D, and E.
Biopsy of the temporal artery (Choice A) is used to diagnose temporal arteritis. Temporal arteritis is a vasculitis of medium and large vessels in elderly patients. Patients commonly present with headache, jaw claudication, and proximal muscle stiffness, and is commonly associated with PMR. This patient does not display any symptoms suggestive of temporal arteritis.
Biopsy of the vastus medialis muscle (Choice B) is used to diagnose inflammatory myopathy, such as polymyositis. Symptoms of polymyositis include proximal muscle weakness, occasionally with increased creatine kinase, but muscle stiffness is not a typical feature. This patient's clinical presentation is more consistent with PMR than polymyositis.
Dopamine transporter (DAT) scan (Choice C) can be used to support a diagnosis of Parkinson disease in patients who present with tremor, rigidity, bradykinesia, or postural instability. This patient's muscle stiffness, pain, and fatigue are not consistent with Parkinson disease.
Duloxetine therapy (Choice D) is a selective serotonin and norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor that is used in treatment of major depressive disorder, generalized anxiety disorder, fibromyalgia, and chronic pain. This patient has generalized anxiety disorder, but a trial of an SSRI or other antidepressant would not address her muscular pain and stiffness.
Prednisone therapy (Choice E) is indicated in the treatment of polymyalgia rheumatica. Low-dose prednisone is indicated in the treatment of polymyalgia rheumatica.
explanation.educationalObjective: Educational Objective: Polymyalgia rheumatica (PMR) is an inflammatory condition in which aching and stiffness develop in the muscles of the hip and shoulder girdles. Low-dose prednisone is the treatment of choice.

text: A 47-year-old man comes to the physician because of severe pain of the left eye and double vision that began after a motor vehicle collision 1 week ago. At the time of the collision, he hit his head but did not lose consciousness. He did not seek medical attention at that time. Examination shows exophthalmos, chemosis, and distention of the orbital and periorbital veins in the left eye. A bruit is heard over the left orbit. There is incomplete abduction of the left eye when looking to the left. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.correct:
Correct Answer: C.

Carotid cavernous fistula (CCF) results from an inappropriate connection between the carotid artery and cavernous sinus, often caused by head trauma, aneurysmal rupture, inflammation, or vascular or connective tissue disease. Arterial pressure in the cavernous sinus results in cranial nerve deficits involving those that traverse the cavernous sinus (III, IV, V₁, V₂, or VI) as a result of extrinsic compression. Other findings can include vascular congestion of the ipsilateral eye and face, exophthalmos, chemosis, unilateral headache, eye pain, visual loss, and an audible bruit on auscultation of the eye. CCFs can be categorized as low-flow fistulas, which result from communication between the cavernous sinus and cavernous arterial branches, or high-flow fistulas, which result from a direct communication between the cavernous sinus and the internal carotid artery. Carotid angiography is the gold standard diagnostic modality, will show flow through the fistula. CT or MR angiography may also be used. First-line treatment is endovascular intervention with embolization or stenting.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answers: A, B, D, and E.
Acute cerebral infarction (Choice A) can present with a sudden change in mental status, upper motor neuron signs, and acute focal neurologic deficits, especially on one side of the body, and may have speech difficulty, dysarthria, or aphasia, depending on the vascular territory involved. Exophthalmos and an orbital bruit are not consistent with acute cerebral infarction.
Arteriovenous malformation (Choice B) describes an abnormal tangle of blood vessels located within the brain. A ruptured arteriovenous malformation may result in a subarachnoid or intracerebral hemorrhage (ICH). ICH appears as a hyperdense collection of blood within the brain parenchyma on CT scan. ICH often presents with focal neurologic deficits based on their location with an associated headache and symptoms of increased intracranial pressure (eg, vomiting, altered mental status).
Epidural hematoma (Choice D) is a collection of blood in the epidural space, typically between the dura mater and the skull. It classically occurs after injury to the middle meningeal artery and can present with an initial lucid interval followed by deterioration of mental status caused by arterial bleeding and hematoma expansion, along with headache, vomiting, agitation, seizure, and coma. Epidural hematomas present on CT scan as a lens-shaped, bi-convex hyperdense collection abutting the skull.
Subarachnoid hemorrhage (Choice E) typically presents with an acute, severe headache along with neck stiffness, photophobia, and possible stupor or coma in severe cases. CT scan of the head typically shows hyperdense blood products in the cerebral sulci and basal cisterns. Intracranial aneurysms are the most common cause of non-traumatic subarachnoid hemorrhage, followed by arteriovenous malformations. It is a neurosurgical emergency that may be fatal if progressive or untreated.
Subdural hematoma (Choice F) results from head trauma and hemorrhage from bridging veins in the subdural space. They present with subacute-onset headache, nausea, and vomiting, and may present with focal neurologic deficits or seizures caused by mass effect. Subdural hematomas appear as a crescent-shaped hyperdense collection in parallel to the curvature of the skull and cross the dural sutures.
explanation.educationalObjective: Educational Objective: Carotid cavernous fistula (CCF) results from an abnormal communication between the carotid artery and cavernous sinus, often caused by head trauma. It presents with exophthalmos, chemosis, orbital congestion, pain, double vision, and audible bruit on auscultation of the eye.

text: After renovating a new home, a 41-year-old woman has burning pain and tingling in her right index, long, and ring fingers, particularly at night. The thenar eminence becomes atrophied. Which of the following structures is most likely to be damaged?

explanation.correct:
Correct Answer: C.

Carpal tunnel syndrome (CTS) results from compression of the median nerve within the carpal tunnel and presents with signs and symptoms including pain, weakness, and paresthesia of the first three digits and the lateral half of the fourth digit, along with positive provocative test maneuvers such as the Tinel or Phalen tests. The carpal tunnel is located anterior to the carpal bones and is bound by the flexor retinaculum. Flexor tendons of the fingers course through the tunnel, along with the median nerve. The space is rigid and does not generally permit expansion. Thus, any inflammatory, toxic, or space-filling lesion may result in compression of the median nerve, leading to pain and paresthesia in the median nerve distribution, along with weakness and atrophy of the thenar eminence that eventually may atrophy. Symptoms are generally worsened at night and improved with shaking of the hands. CTS is frequently associated with pregnancy, hypothyroidism, rheumatoid arthritis, acromegaly, amyloidosis, ganglion cysts, obesity, and acute trauma to the wrist with impingement, or chronic repetitive stress and scar formation following a wrist injury. Patients with mild symptoms can be treated conservatively with wrist splinting. Wrist splints are typically worn at night but may also be used during the day. Resisting daily activities that involve repetitive wrist motion and limits activities that increase pressure within the carpal tunnel. Patients who have persistent or severe symptoms or muscular weakness may require surgical decompression.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answers: A, B, D, and E.
Injuries to the radial artery (Choice A) and ulnar artery (Choice B) would not result in chronic neurologic dysfunction or muscle atrophy.
The radial nerve (Choice D) courses posterior to the humerus at the midshaft within the radial groove. It is often injured in displaced midshaft humerus fractures, with resulting wrist drop, and hand. The radial nerve supplies motor fibers to the triceps brachii and the extensor muscles within the posterior compartment of the forearm, and sensory fibers to the dorsum of the forearm, wrist, and hand.
The ulnar nerve (Choice E) supplies motor fibers to the intrinsic muscles of the hand, and sensation over the anteromedial ring and small fingers and medial palm including hypothenar eminence. Damage to the roots or to the nerve along its course (eg, in the cubital tunnel) can result in motor dysfunction of the fingers, and numbness or paresthesia involving the medial aspect of the upper extremity.
explanation.educationalObjective: Educational Objective: Carpal tunnel syndrome results from compression of the median nerve within the carpal tunnel and presents with signs and symptoms including pain, weakness, and paresthesia of the first three digits and the lateral half of the fourth digit, along with weakness and atrophy of the thenar eminence.

text: A 21-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by her mother 1 hour after a 30-minute episode of generalized shaking. One week ago, the patient had a similar episode that began while she was at home watching television with her mother. At that time, the patient fell to the ground and had irregular jerking of her hands and legs. During the episode, her eyes were open and she was occasionally able to respond to her mother's commands. The episode lasted 10 minutes. The patient has had three similar episodes over the past week. She has smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for 6 years. She drinks three to four 12-oz beers weekly. She does not use illicit drugs. She works as a home health aide. She has been under great stress recently following her breakup with her fiancé. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical, neurologic, and mental status examinations disclose no abnormalities. CT scan of the head discloses no abnormalities. During a video EEG, the patient has a typical seizure; there are no epileptiform EEG changes during the episode. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

explanation.correct:
Correct Answer: A.

Conversion disorder, also known as functional neurologic symptom disorder, involves neurologic symptoms such as sensory or motor dysfunction that are not fully explained by objective findings on physical examination or imaging. Functional neurologic symptom disorder is often related to psychological factors, such as stress, trauma, or anxiety, and is thought to represent the subconscious conversion of emotional distress into physical symptoms. The shaking episodes described in this patient appear to be non-epileptic based on the lack of epileptiform changes on EEG. The presence of irregular jerking, eye opening, ability to respond to commands during the episode, and lack of postictal confusion are all features that suggest against epileptic seizures. Cognitive behavior therapy (CBT) is a type of psychotherapy that focuses on changing thought and behavior patterns and is an effective treatment for functional neurologic symptom disorder. CBT can help patients identify triggers for their symptoms and develop coping strategies.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answers: B, C, D, and E.
Long-term EEG monitoring (Choice B) can be used to capture a typical seizure and confirm whether it is epileptic or non-epileptic. However, since the patient has already had a typical seizure captured on video EEG, and it was determined to be non-epileptic, further EEG monitoring is unlikely to be helpful.
Measurement of serum prolactin concentration (Choice C) can be used to differentiate between epileptic and non-epileptic seizures. Serum prolactin levels typically rise after an epileptic seizure but not after a non-epileptic seizure. This test could have been useful in the initial workup, but the video EEG has already provided more definitive information.
MRI of the brain (Choice D) would be appropriate if there was concern for a structural lesion causing epileptic seizures or other neurologic symptoms. The CT scan was normal, and the video EEG findings suggest against epileptic seizures, making an MRI less likely to be helpful at this time.
Oxcarbazepine therapy (Choice E) is an anticonvulsant medication used to treat epileptic seizures. Since the patient's shaking episodes appear to be non-epileptic, anticonvulsant therapy is not indicated and may cause unnecessary side effects.
explanation.educationalObjective: Educational Objective: Functional neurologic symptom disorder, also known as conversion disorder, involves neurologic symptoms that are not explained by objective medical findings and are thought to be related to psychological factors. Non-epileptic seizures are a common presentation of functional neurologic symptom disorder. Treatment often involves psychotherapy, such as cognitive behavior therapy.

text: An 82-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 1-day history of visual loss in the right eye. He has had no eye pain. He has had approximately five episodes of visual loss in the right eye over the past 3 weeks that lasted less than 1 hour. He has hypertension and hyperlipidemia. Medications include captopril and simvastatin. His pulse is 85/min, and blood pressure is 135/90 mm Hg. Corrected visual acuity is 20/40 on the left and limited to hand motion on the right. There is a prominent afferent pupillary defect in the right eye. Funduscopic findings of the right eye are shown. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.correct:
Correct Answer: B.

Central retinal artery occlusion (CRAO) is an ocular emergency characterized by acute, painless vision loss in one eye. It is typically caused by an embolus occluding the central retinal artery, which supplies blood to the inner retina. Risk factors for CRAO include carotid artery disease, cardiac emboli (from atrial fibrillation or valvular disease), hypertension, diabetes, and hyperlipidemia. Funduscopic examination typically shows a pale retina with a cherry-red spot in the macula, although the fundus may appear normal in the early stages. A relative afferent pupillary defect (rAPD) is usually present. CRAO is a medical emergency because it is a form of ischemic stroke and indicates a high risk for stroke to the brain. Patients with CRAO should be evaluated immediately for an embolic source with carotid duplex ultrasound, echocardiogram, and potentially cardiac monitoring. Treatment focuses on lowering intraocular pressure to potentially improve blood flow and managing underlying risk factors.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answers: A, C, D, and E.
Age-related macular degeneration (Choice A) is a chronic condition affecting the macula, the central part of the retina responsible for sharp central vision. It typically causes gradual, painless vision loss, often with distortion of straight lines. It is not characterized by acute, episodic vision loss or a pale retina.
Central retinal vein occlusion (Choice C) is caused by a blockage of the central retinal vein, leading to blood backup and hemorrhage in the retina. Funduscopic examination shows dilated and tortuous retinal veins, scattered hemorrhages, and cotton wool spots (nerve fiber layer infarcts). Vision loss is typically sudden and painless, but the fundus appearance is distinct from CRAO.
Hypertensive retinopathy (Choice D) refers to changes in the retinal blood vessels caused by chronic high blood pressure. Findings can include narrowed arteries, arteriovenous nicking, hemorrhages, exudates, and papilledema in severe cases. While this patient has hypertension, the acute, episodic vision loss and pale retina with a rAPD are more consistent with CRAO.
Ischemic optic neuropathy (Choice E) is caused by decreased blood flow to the optic nerve and presents with acute, painless vision loss and a rAPD. There are two types: anterior ischemic optic neuropathy (AION), which affects the optic nerve head and causes disc edema, and posterior ischemic optic neuropathy (PION), which affects the optic nerve behind the eye and does not cause disc edema. AION is associated with risk factors like diabetes, hypertension, and sleep apnea. Arteritic AION is caused by giant cell arteritis and is a medical emergency requiring prompt steroid treatment to prevent vision loss in the other eye. While ischemic optic neuropathy can cause sudden vision loss and a rAPD, the fundus appearance of a pale retina with a cherry-red spot is characteristic of CRAO.
explanation.educationalObjective: Educational Objective: Central retinal artery occlusion (CRAO) is an ocular emergency caused by embolic occlusion of the central retinal artery. Patients present with acute, painless vision loss and a pale retina with a cherry-red spot in the macula. Evaluation should focus on identifying the embolic source due to the high risk of associated stroke.

text: A 47-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by her husband 45 minutes after the sudden onset of a severe headache. She has a 25-year history of occasional throbbing headaches. The headaches occur once or twice annually, are associated with nausea and vomiting, and are relieved with sleep. She has hypertension treated with enalapril. On arrival, she can answer questions but is intermittently listless and agitated. Her temperature is 37.7°C (99.8°F), pulse is 120/min, respirations are 24/min, and blood pressure is 154/96 mm Hg. On examination, the left pupil is larger than the right. The left eye deviates slightly downward and outward. The patient resists flexion of the neck and exhibits pain when the neck is flexed. Muscle tone in the right upper and lower extremities is increased. Deep tendon reflexes on the right side are increased. Babinski sign is present on the right. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.correct:
Correct Answer: D.

A ruptured intracranial aneurysm is the most common cause of non-traumatic subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH). SAH occurs when there is bleeding into the subarachnoid space, often from a ruptured aneurysm or arteriovenous malformation. Patients typically present with an acute, severe headache, often described as the "worst headache of my life," along with nausea, vomiting, photophobia, and neck stiffness (meningismus). Aneurysms located at the junction of the posterior communicating artery and the internal carotid artery can compress the oculomotor nerve (cranial nerve III), leading to an ipsilateral oculomotor palsy with a dilated pupil (parasympathetic fibers affected) and impaired eye movements (downward and outward deviation, ptosis). Hemiparesis with upper motoneuron signs on the contralateral side can also occur due to compression of the cerebral peduncle or internal capsule. Diagnosis is typically made with a non-contrast CT scan of the head, which shows hyperdense blood in the basal cisterns and sulci. If the CT scan is negative but suspicion remains high, a lumbar puncture can be performed, which may show red blood cells or xanthochromia (yellow discoloration due to hemoglobin breakdown). SAH is a neurosurgical emergency and requires prompt management to prevent re-bleeding, vasospasm, and hydrocephalus.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answers: A, B, C, and E.

Carotid artery dissection with embolization (Choice A) can cause ischemic stroke symptoms, including headache, neck pain, and focal neurologic deficits (depending on the area of the brain supplied by the affected artery). Oculomotor nerve palsy would be atypical in this setting unless the dissection extends intracranially to compress the nerve.

Hypertensive intracranial hemorrhage (Choice B) typically occurs in patients with chronic hypertension and involves bleeding into the brain parenchyma, commonly in the basal ganglia, thalamus, pons, or cerebellum. While ICH can cause acute headache and focal neurologic deficits, it would be less likely to cause a specific isolated oculomotor nerve palsy with pupillary involvement compared to a posterior communicating artery aneurysm.

Meningoencephalitis (Choice C), inflammation of the brain and meninges, typically presents with fever, headache, altered mental status, and meningismus. Focal neurologic deficits, including cranial nerve palsies, can occur. However, the acute onset severe headache, dilated pupil, and specific oculomotor palsy pattern are more characteristic of SAH from an aneurysm compressing the nerve.

Vertebrobasilar migraine (Choice E) is a type of migraine with aura symptoms that originate from the vertebrobasilar circulation. Symptoms can include dizziness, vertigo, double vision, speech difficulty, and sometimes headache. While double vision can occur, a dilated pupil and specific oculomotor palsy are not typical features of migraine.
explanation.educationalObjective: Educational Objective: Ruptured saccular aneurysms are a common cause of non-traumatic subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH). Aneurysms arising from the posterior communicating artery can compress the oculomotor nerve (cranial nerve III), leading to an ipsilateral oculomotor palsy characterized by a dilated pupil and impaired eye movements (downward and outward deviation, ptosis). SAH presents with an acute, severe headache, meningismus, and often focal neurologic deficits depending on the location and complications.

text:
A 47-year-old man has to quit his job as an automobile mechanic because of hand weakness and unsteadiness. He has bilateral wristdrop and upper limb ataxia. Laboratory studies show:
Hematocrit 34%
Mean corpuscular volume 76 μm³
Serum creatinine 2 mg/dL
Chronic exposure to which of the following substances is the most likely explanation for these findings?

explanation.correct:
Correct Answer: C.

Chronic exposure to lead is the most likely explanation for this patient's symptoms of motor weakness (wristdrop), ataxia, and cognitive dysfunction, given his occupation as an automobile mechanic and laboratory findings of microcytic anemia and renal dysfunction. Lead poisoning affects multiple organ systems. Neurologic manifestations include cognitive dysfunction, encephalopathy, hearing loss, peripheral neuropathy (often predominantly motor, leading to wristdrop or footdrop), and ataxia. Gastrointestinal symptoms can include abdominal pain, constipation, and vomiting. Lead poisoning can also cause renal tubular dysfunction and interstitial nephritis, leading to increased serum creatinine and impaired renal function. Hematologic effects include microcytic, hypochromic anemia due to impaired heme synthesis. Diagnosis is confirmed with measurement of lead concentration in the blood or, for chronic exposure, in bone. Treatment involves chelation therapy with agents such as edetate calcium disodium or dimercaprol. Sources of lead should be investigated and eliminated.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answers: A, B, D, and E.
Arsenic (Choice A) poisoning is uncommon and can cause dermatitis, skin cancer, neuropathy, hepatotoxicity, and, in severe cases, systemic toxicity and death. Acute large exposure to arsenic can cause gastrointestinal distress. QT prolongation that may progress to torsades de pointes, cardiac arrhythmias, shock, and acute respiratory distress syndrome.
Cadmium (Choice B) poisoning may occur in the industrial setting among workers involved in electroplating, metallurgical industries, and battery production. Exposure typically occurs via inhalation and presents with fever, chills, myalgia, chest pain, cough, shortness of breath, pneumonitis, and pulmonary edema.
Mercury (Choice D) poisoning may occur in the industrial setting among workers involved in the manufacture of thermometers, mirrors, fluorescent lighting, and gold. Patients present with headache, ataxia, tremor, hearing and vision loss, insomnia, peripheral neuropathy, and paresthesias.
Thallium (Choice E) poisoning may occur through transcutaneous exposure or via inhalation or ingestion. Thallium was previously widely used as a rodenticide, but thallium poisoning is now rare in the developed world, although it is still used in the manufacture of semiconductors, electronics, and optics. Acute exposure presents with abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting, while chronic exposure presents with neurologic and psychiatric symptoms of headache, altered mental status, anxiety, psychosis, ataxia, tremor, and painful peripheral neuropathy and distal weakness.
explanation.educationalObjective: Educational Objective: Neurologic manifestations of lead poisoning include cognitive dysfunction, encephalopathy, hearing loss, and peripheral neuropathy. Distal motor neuropathy causes wristdrop or footdrop. Lead poisoning also causes renal tubular dysfunction and microcytic anemia.

text: A 27-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 1-day history of difficulty speaking and moving. Seven days ago she began a course of mitoxantrone and high doses of cytarabine (ara-C) for a recurrence of acute myeloid leukemia. She has been lethargic and mildly confused for the past 24 hours. She is 140 cm (4 ft 7 in) tall and weighs 47 kg (103 lb); BMI is 24 kg/m². Her temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 72/min, respirations are 14/min, and blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg. Examination shows nystagmus and ataxic eye movements. Muscle strength is full, but there is marked ataxia in all extremities. She is unable to maintain her balance sitting or standing unsupported. She is somnolent but can respond appropriately to questions and instructions. Her speech is irregular in rate and volume. Language function and memory are normal. She can calculate well, and her abstract reasoning is intact. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.E: Spread of leukemia to the central nervous system

explanation.correct:
Correct Answer: B.

Acute-onset neurologic findings in the setting of recent chemotherapy are concerning for drug toxicity. Specifically, nystagmus, ataxia, impaired coordination, postural instability, and dysarthria or other speech abnormalities, in the setting of intact strength and higher cognitive functions are indicative of cerebellar dysfunction. Cytarabine, a pyrimidine analog, can cause loss of Purkinje cells in the cerebellum, leading to acute cerebellar syndrome. Risk factors include underlying neurologic dysfunction and impaired hepatic or renal function. Patients who present with acute cerebellar syndrome should discontinue cytarabine immediately. Some may experience permanent deficits, while others may experience spontaneous resolution.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answers: A, C, D, and E.
Cerebral infarction (Choice A) can present with a sudden change in mental status, upper motor neuron signs, and acute focal neurologic deficits, especially on one side of the body, and may have speech difficulty, dysarthria, or aphasia, depending on the vascular territory involved. However, acute-onset cerebellar signs in the setting of new course of cytarabine are more likely caused by drug toxicity.
Encephalitis (Choice C) presents with fever, altered mental status, and neurologic abnormalities, such as seizure, aphasia, hemiparesis, or cranial nerve deficits. Meningoencephalitis is commonly associated with nuchal rigidity and signs of central nervous system infection, which this patient does not demonstrate, making it a less likely cause of her presentation.
Intracranial hemorrhage (Choice D) appears as a hyperdense collection of blood within the brain parenchyma on CT scan. Intracranial hemorrhage often presents with focal neurologic deficits based on its location with an associated headache and symptoms of increased intracranial pressure (eg, vomiting, altered mental status). This patient does not demonstrate any signs of increased intracranial pressure.
Spread of leukemia to the central nervous system (Choice E), also called leukemic meningitis, is usually asymptomatic. When it does occur, signs and symptoms vary widely depending on the location of spread, and can include headache, signs of increased intracranial pressure, behavioral changes, and focal weakness. Isolated cerebellar signs in the setting of recently started cytarabine is more consistent with drug toxicity.
explanation.educationalObjective: Educational Objective: Cytarabine, a pyrimidine analog, can cause loss of Purkinje cells in the cerebellum, leading to acute cerebellar syndrome. Features include nystagmus, ataxia, impaired coordination, postural instability, and dysarthria or other speech abnormalities. Strength, sensation, and higher cognitive functions are intact. Patients who present with acute cerebellar syndrome should discontinue cytarabine immediately. Some may experience permanent deficits, while others may experience spontaneous resolution.

text: A 67-year-old woman with a 15-year history of Parkinson disease comes to the office because of increasing involuntary movements over the past 2 years. She also has had nausea from her medication regimen of carbidopa-levodopa and ropinirole; attempts at medication adjustment have not been successful. She also has a long-standing history of cervical and lumbar stenosis treated with diclofenac and physical therapy. Her pulse is 70/min and blood pressure is 110/60 mm Hg while sitting; pulse is 75/min and blood pressure is 80/50 mm Hg while standing. Her speech is barely audible. Examination shows constant writhing movements of the mouth, neck, and limbs. She walks with her neck flexed and takes small steps with a decreased arm swing. Mental status examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate surgical treatment?

explanation.correct:
Correct Answer: E.

Parkinson disease is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder caused by destruction of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra. Symptoms include bradykinesia, rigidity, resting tremor, and postural instability. As the disease progresses, patients often require increasing doses of dopamine agonists or levodopa, which can cause adverse effects such as nausea and involuntary movements (dyskinesias), as in this patient. Dyskinesias can be troublesome and may not resolve with medication adjustment. For patients with disabling dyskinesias or motor fluctuations despite optimal medical management, surgical options such as deep brain stimulation or lesioning procedures may be considered. Pallidotomy is a lesioning procedure that targets the globus pallidus internus, a component of the basal ganglia involved in motor control. By creating a lesion in this area, pallidotomy can help reduce dyskinesias and motor fluctuations in patients with Parkinson disease.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answers: A, B, C, D, F, and G.
Anterior temporal lobectomy (Choice A) is an effective surgical treatment for intractable temporal lobe epilepsy. It would not help with severe motor symptoms of Parkinson disease.
Carotid endarterectomy (Choice B) is a treatment for atherosclerotic disease of the carotid arteries. It is indicated for patients who have had a stroke or transient ischemic attack or are symptomatic with hemodynamically significant stenosis.
Cervical laminectomy (Choice C) or lumbar laminectomy (Choice D) are spine surgery procedures used to decompress the spinal cord or nerve roots in the setting of spinal stenosis or disc herniation. While this patient has a history of spinal stenosis, these procedures would not address her dyskinesias related to Parkinson disease.
Thymectomy (Choice F) is reserved for patients with tumors of the thymus or with myasthenia gravis. Myasthenia gravis characteristically presents with generalized weakness and ptosis that worsens throughout the day.
Ventriculoperitoneal shunting (Choice G) is used to remove excess cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) in patients with hydrocephalus, either from lesions obstructing CSF outflow or ones that impair CSF reabsorption.
explanation.educationalObjective: Educational Objective: For patients with Parkinson disease who experience disabling dyskinesias or motor fluctuations despite optimal medical management, surgical options such as deep brain stimulation or lesioning procedures like pallidotomy may be considered. These procedures target components of the basal ganglia involved in motor control to help reduce symptoms.

text:
A previously healthy 37-year-old man is brought to the physician by his wife because of bizarre behavior for 2 days. He is very excitable and jumps when surprised. His wife says that he has been experiencing visual hallucinations. He refuses to drink liquids. For 3 days prior to this behavior, he had headache and sleepiness. Two months ago, he returned from a hunting trip in the mountains. His temperature is 40°C (104°F). Examination shows an inability to accurately answer questions and unusual behaviors such as picking at the air. He seems unable to swallow his secretions, and he is drooling. A CT scan of the brain shows no abnormalities. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis shows:
Glucose 70 mg/dL
Protein 85 mg/dL
Leukocyte count 150/mm³
Erythrocyte count none

explanation.correct:
Correct Answer: H.

This patient presents with severe, acute-onset neurologic symptoms including bizarre behavior, hallucinations, refusal to drink, inability to swallow secretions (hydrophobia/difficulty swallowing), and hypersalivation, preceded by headache and sleepiness, in the setting of a recent hunting trip and fever, is highly suggestive of rabies. Rabies is a viral encephalitis caused by infection with the rabies virus, a negative-sense, single-stranded RNA virus belonging to the family of Rhabdoviridae. The virus is typically transmitted through direct contact with saliva of an infected animal, most commonly through a bite, although transmission through scratches or mucosal exposure can also occur. In the United States, the most common source of rabies virus infection is wild animals, including bats, raccoons, skunks, and foxes. The incubation period is typically 4 to 12 weeks but can range from days to years. Initial symptoms are nonspecific and include fever, headache, malaise, and fatigue. Neurologic symptoms develop rapidly and can progress to agitation, photophobia, hydrophobia, drooling, and fevers. Once neurologic symptoms develop, rabies is almost universally fatal. Postmortem diagnosis can be made by identifying Negri bodies (intracytoplasmic inclusions) in neuronal tissue. Prevention after exposure includes prompt irrigation and wound care, administration of rabies immune globulin, and administration of rabies vaccine series. Standard care for pets, including vaccination, is essential for preventing rabies transmission. If the species of animal that transmitted rabies is unknown, the patient should be treated as if the animal was infected. Immune globulin should be administered locally into the wound and intramuscularly.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answers: A, B, C, D, E, F, G, and I.
Arbovirus (Choice A) refers to a group of viruses that are transmitted by arthropods (eg, ticks, mosquitoes). West Nile virus is an example, and patients typically present with fever, headache, myalgia, nausea, vomiting, and a non-specific maculopapular rash distributed symmetrically over the extremities and trunk. Meningoencephalitis is associated with acute flaccid paralysis or tremors, and cognitive dysfunction.
Borrelia burgdorferi (Choice B) is transmitted by Ixodes ticks and causes Lyme disease. Neurologic manifestations of Lyme disease include meningitis, cranial nerve palsies (especially facial palsy), radiculopathy, and peripheral neuropathy. Fever, headache, and malaise are common symptoms of acute Lyme disease, and it may present with erythema migrans, a characteristic skin rash. Rabies is not associated with tick bites.
Cytomegalovirus (Choice C) is a herpesvirus that can cause mononucleosis-like syndrome with fever, fatigue, and cervical lymphadenopathy in immunocompetent individuals. It can cause encephalitis in immunocompromised patients, but this is less common. Rabies is not a herpesvirus.
Enterovirus (Choice D) is a common cause of viral meningitis and encephalitis, particularly in children. Symptoms include fever, headache, meningismus, and sometimes rash or focal neurologic deficits. Rabies is not an enterovirus.
Herpes simplex virus (HSV) (Choice E) can cause encephalitis, typically in the temporal lobe. Symptoms include fever, headache, altered mental status, and neurologic abnormalities, such as seizure, aphasia, hemiparesis, or cranial nerve deficits. Rabies is not an HSV.
HIV (Choice F) infection can cause HIV encephalitis, particularly in the late stages of the disease. Symptoms include cognitive dysfunction, motor weakness, and neuropsychiatric symptoms. Rabies is not HIV.
Listeria monocytogenes (Choice G) is a bacterium that can cause meningoencephalitis, particularly in patients at the extremes of age or those who are immunocompromised. It presents with fever, headache, nuchal rigidity, altered mental status, and focal neurologic deficits. Rabies is not caused by bacteria.
Rickettia rickettsii (Choice I) is a bacterium that is transmitted by ticks and causes Rocky Mountain spotted fever. Symptoms include fever, headache, myalgia, rash, and in severe cases, neurologic complications such as encephalitis or seizures. Rabies is not a rickettsial infection.
Toxoplasma gondii (Choice J) is an intracellular protozoan parasite that can cause cerebral toxoplasmosis in immunocompromised patients. It presents with seizures, focal neurologic deficits, and systemic symptoms. Rabies is not a protozoan.
explanation.educationalObjective: Educational Objective: Rabies is a viral encephalitis transmitted by the rabies virus. It presents with acute, rapidly progressive neurologic symptoms including agitation, hallucinations, hydrophobia (difficulty swallowing), drooling, and fevers, preceded by nonspecific symptoms such as headache and sleepiness. Rabies is almost universally fatal once neurologic symptoms develop. Prevention after exposure involves prompt wound care, administration of rabies immune globulin, and rabies vaccine series.

text:
A 17-year-old boy with sickle cell disease is brought to the clinic by his father because of a 4-week history of progressive right hip pain. Initially, the pain was intermittent and the patient rated it as a 5 on a 10-point scale; however, during the past 2 weeks it has become constant, and he now rates the pain as a 9 on a 10-point scale. The pain is not worsened by sneezing, coughing, or lifting objects. He was diagnosed with sickle cell disease at age 3 years; his disease course has been complicated by frequent bouts of dactylitis and pneumonia. He has had two blood transfusions done 9 and 6 years ago, respectively. He is taking acetaminophen for the hip pain, but it has provided minimal relief. Family history is remarkable for sickle cell disease in his mother, brother, and two sisters, and for sickle cell trait in his father. The patient appears to be in moderate distress. Temperature is 36.6°C (98.6°F), pulse is 98/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 114/68 mm Hg. Physical examination shows a decreased range of motion to hip flexion and abduction. There is no pain on palpation of the lateral aspect of the hip. Muscle strength is 5/5 throughout, and muscle tone is symmetric. Deep tendon reflexes are 1+ in the lower extremities bilaterally. Sensation to pinprick is normal over the lower extremities. Results of laboratory studies are shown:
Hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL
Hematocrit 28.7%
Leukocyte count 7200/mm³
Platelet count 212,000/mm³
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 13 mm/h
Serum
C-reactive protein 0.2 mg/L
Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's pain?

options.A: Avascular necrosis of the femoral head

explanation.correct:
Correct Answer: A.

Avascular necrosis, or osteonecrosis, results from impaired vascular supply to the bone with painful degeneration and loss of structural integrity over time. Decreased blood flow may result from direct injury to the blood vessels or from systemic conditions that cause impaired microcirculation, such as sickle cell disease, systemic lupus erythematosus, chronic steroid use, or radiation therapy. Symptoms include localized pain that worsens with activity and weight-bearing, stiffness, and limited range of motion. In sickle cell disease, bone ischemia is a frequent complication, particularly affecting the femoral head and leading to avascular necrosis of the hip. Symptoms may include hip pain, limp, and restricted hip movement. Diagnosis is made with imaging studies, including x-ray, CT scan, or MRI. MRI is the gold standard for diagnosis, showing areas of decreased signal intensity on T1-weighted images and increased signal intensity on T2-weighted images due to bone marrow edema and necrosis. Treatment options depend on the stage of avascular necrosis and can range from conservative management with pain control and limited weight-bearing to surgical intervention, such as core decompression, osteotomy, or total hip replacement.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answers: B, C, D, E, and F.

L2 radiculopathy (Choice B) would lead to pain, numbness, and tingling in the L2 dermatome, which travels from the lateral to medial thigh. While pain is present in this patient, it does not follow a radicular distribution, and the range of motion limitations and hip pain are more suggestive of hip joint pathology.

Osteoarthritis of the hip (Choice C) is a degenerative joint disease that commonly affects older adults. Patients present with chronic hip pain that worsens with activity and improves with rest, stiffness, and limited range of motion. While osteoarthritis is a possibility in this patient given his age, avascular necrosis is a more common cause of hip pain in patients with sickle cell disease.

Osteomyelitis of the acetabulum (Choice D) is an infection of the bone. It typically presents with pain, fever, and localized swelling and tenderness. While this patient has pain and a history of sickle cell disease (a risk factor for osteomyelitis), he does not have fever or localized swelling, making osteomyelitis less likely.

Osteosarcoma of the femur (Choice E) is a primary malignant tumor of bone that affects adolescents and elderly patients. Patients may present with localized pain, a palpable mass, and weight loss. While this patient has pain and weight loss, the localized hip joint pain and limited range of motion are more suggestive of avascular necrosis. Osteosarcoma would typically appear as a destructive bone lesion on x-ray with periosteal new bone formation.

Trochanteric bursitis (Choice F) classically presents with lateral hip pain that is exacerbated by activity. Physical examination shows point tenderness over the greater trochanter and increased pain with external rotation of the hip as a result of compression of the bursa. The patient's pain is not localized to the lateral aspect of the hip and is more consistent with hip joint pathology.
explanation.educationalObjective: Educational Objective: Avascular necrosis, or osteonecrosis, results from impaired vascular supply to the bone. In sickle cell disease, bone ischemia is a frequent complication, particularly affecting the femoral head and leading to avascular necrosis of the hip. Symptoms include localized pain that worsens with activity and weight-bearing, stiffness, and limited range of motion. Diagnosis is made with imaging studies, such as MRI.

text: A 67-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department 24 hours after the onset of double vision, pain behind the right eye, and mild nausea. She describes her double vision as seeing objects side by side. She has hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, peripheral neuropathy, and chronic renal insufficiency. Medications include fosinopril and glyburide. Vital signs are within normal limits. On examination, her double vision is most pronounced when she looks to the right. If she covers either eye, the double vision resolves. The pupils are 3 mm and normally reactive to light. When the patient looks to the right, the right eye does not abduct. Achilles tendon reflexes are absent. Sensation to pinprick is decreased in a stocking distribution over the feet. An MRI of the brain is most likely to show which of the following?

explanation.correct:
Correct Answer: E.

Abducens nerve palsy is characterized by impaired abduction of the ipsilateral eye, leading to horizontal double vision that is most pronounced when looking towards the affected side. Isolated abducens nerve palsy in older adults with vascular risk factors such as diabetes and hypertension is commonly caused by microvascular ischemia of the nerve. Microvascular cranial nerve palsies are typically painless and resolve spontaneously within months. Other causes of abducens nerve palsy include compression from tumors, inflammation, infection, or increased intracranial pressure. In this patient with diabetes and hypertension, a microvascular abducens nerve palsy is the most likely diagnosis. While imaging is often performed to rule out other causes, in patients with isolated abducens nerve palsy and vascular risk factors, normal findings on MRI are common.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answers: A, B, C, and D.
Acute temporal lobe hemorrhage (Choice A) would be more likely to have more pronounced neurologic deficits, such as aphasia or hemiparesis, in addition to headache and potential altered mental status. Diplopia that is centrally mediated is typically bilateral, whereas diplopia that resolves when the patient covers one eye is likely due to an ocular cause.
Enhancing meninges (Choice B) on MRI would be suggestive of an infectious or inflammatory process such as meningitis. While meningitis can present with focal neurologic deficits, patients typically have other signs or symptoms such as fever and nuchal rigidity.
Infarct in the right medulla (Choice C) can lead to lateral medullary syndrome, which can present with ipsilateral ataxia of extremity movements, gait ataxia, Horner syndrome, and can be associated with nystagmus and dysarthria. Isolated abducens nerve palsy is not typical with medullary infarcts.
An orbital tumor (Choice D) would be unlikely to result in abducens nerve palsy. It would more likely affect ipsilateral extraocular muscles through local mass effect.
explanation.educationalObjective: Educational Objective: Abducens nerve palsy is characterized by impaired abduction of the ipsilateral eye and horizontal double vision. Isolated abducens nerve palsy in older adults with vascular risk factors is commonly caused by microvascular ischemia and often presents with normal findings on MRI.

text: A 32-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department 3 hours after the onset of confusion. She appears agitated. She mistakes the attending physician for her brother and insists that he call their father. She admits to drinking regularly since the age of 22 years. Her last drink was 4 ounces of vodka 72 hours ago. Her pulse is 110/min, and blood pressure is 170/110 mm Hg. Physical examination shows ataxia and a three cycle/sec bilateral hand tremor.
